coredata.dc:title: Tri-channel tubular lanthanide nanocomposites for multimodal anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jun
Zhu, Tinghui
Chen, Xiangzhen
Zhao, Dan
Li, Yongxin
Zhang, Lina
Bi, Ning
Gou, Jian
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Integrating multiple signals with different excitation wavelength dependence and temperature response properties into a single nanomaterial to construct multimodal anti-counterfeiting materials is still challenging. In this research, an exciting lanthanide hybrid tri-channel tubular anti-counterfeiting nanomaterial was fabricated by fixing NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+ up-conversion nanoparticles and Cit-Eu-TC complex on the surface of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes, which can be further used to make anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink and hydrogel. The hidden three-mode anti-counterfeiting patterns showed bright green, red, and blue-violet fluorescence under 254 nm, 365, and 980 nm excitation, respectively. More importantly, under the co-irradiation of 254 and 365 nm light, the patterns prepared by the anti-counterfeiting ink showed mesmerizing dynamic anti-counterfeiting performance under the stimulation of developer and temperature. With the developer's dosage increase or temperature change, the color of anti-counterfeiting patterns changed obviously. The combination of this dynamic stimulus-responsive anti-counterfeiting feature and the above three-channel static anti-counterfeiting feature might bring about multi-mode static-dynamic anti-counterfeiting performance, significantly improving the difficulty of forgery and was expected to be widely used in the field of confidential information and file anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tri-mode luminescence
Lanthanide nanocomposite
Multi-color fluorescence
Anti counterfeiting
Fluorescent hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of multi-mode fluorescent anti-counterfeit nanocomposites
Preparation of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink and hydrogel
Morphology and structure characterization of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC
Research on three-channel static anti-counterfeiting performance

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168 , No. 51773052 , and No. 21805071 ), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province ( 222300420047 ), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University ( T2021-2 ), and the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) NaYF4:Yb/Tm, (b) YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb, (c) YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC, (d) magnified TEM images of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC (insets are HRTEM images of NaYF4:Yb/Tm, YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb and YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC).
SEM images of Ce/11.4%Tb (a) and Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC (b), enlarged SEM image (c) and corresponding elemental mapping (d) of Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC.

(a) PXRD patterns of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb (green), NaYF4:Yb/Tm (red), and YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC (blue). (b) XPS survey of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb (black) and YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC (red). Detailed Eu 3 d (c), Tm 4 d (d), and Yb 4 d (e) spectra of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC, (f) Tb 3 d spectra of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb (black) and YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC (red).

(a) Excitation spectrum (blue) and emission spectra (black and red) of related samples under excitation with 260 nm (inset: corresponding fluorescence photos under 254 nm UV lamp). (b) Fluorescence stability of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC at 545 nm under excitation at 260 nm with different temperatures (5–40 °C). (c) Emission spectra of related samples under excitation with 380 nm (inset: corresponding fluorescence photos under 365 nm UV lamp), (d) energy transfer mechanism diagram of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC with UV excitation at 380 nm. (e) Upconversion luminescence emission spectra of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC under excitation with 980 nm (inset: corresponding fluorescence photos under 980 nm NIR laser excitation), (f) energy transfer mechanism diagram of upconversion emission for YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC.
(a) YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC and green commercial ink labeled (a) swan, (b) owl, (c) birds, (d) mermaid and (e) apple tree security photos.
Photographs of anti-counterfeiting hydrogels labeled with YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC under natural light, 254 nm, 365 nm, and 980 nm NIR laser excitation.
Dynamic anti-counterfeit effect of aircraft (a) and pigeon (b) patterns in 254 nm and 365 nm channel and dual channel under the stimulus of developer and temperature.
Schematic diagram of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC triple-mode luminescent material.

head.ce:title: Tri-channel tubular lanthanide nanocomposites for multimodal anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integrating multiple signals with different excitation wavelength dependence and temperature response properties into a single nanomaterial to construct multimodal anti-counterfeiting materials is still challenging. In this research, an exciting lanthanide hybrid tri-channel tubular anti-counterfeiting nanomaterial was fabricated by fixing NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+ up-conversion nanoparticles and Cit-Eu-TC complex on the surface of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes, which can be further used to make anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink and hydrogel. The hidden three-mode anti-counterfeiting patterns showed bright green, red, and blue-violet fluorescence under 254 nm, 365, and 980 nm excitation, respectively. More importantly, under the co-irradiation of 254 and 365 nm light, the patterns prepared by the anti-counterfeiting ink showed mesmerizing dynamic anti-counterfeiting performance under the stimulation of developer and temperature. With the developer's dosage increase or temperature change, the color of anti-counterfeiting patterns changed obviously. The combination of this dynamic stimulus-responsive anti-counterfeiting feature and the above three-channel static anti-counterfeiting feature might bring about multi-mode static-dynamic anti-counterfeiting performance, significantly improving the difficulty of forgery and was expected to be widely used in the field of confidential information and file anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The fake phenomenon has gradually become an increasingly severe problem, which has posed a serious challenge to companies, governments, and customers for a long time [1]. Therefore, developing high-end anti-counterfeiting technology is particularly significant in protecting essential and valuable items that cannot be imitated, such as nameplates, confidential documents, high-end necessities, documents, and bills [2]. Many anti-counterfeiting technologies have been developed in the past decades, including the recent magnetic response plasma safety label and luminescent materials [3–10]. The anti-counterfeiting technology derived from a series of luminescent materials has been widely concerned in anti-counterfeiting because of its rich fluorescence colors and unique optical properties [1,11]. Some luminescent materials, including organic dye molecules [12], semiconductor quantum dots [13], carbon dots [14,15], and persistent nanomaterials [16], have been developed and applied to anti-counterfeiting. However, whether the fluorescence emission of optical materials is rich in color and whether the excitation wavelength is adjustable plays a decisive role in the encoding and decoding of anti-counterfeiting signals [1,2]. The traditional fluorescent anti-counterfeiting pattern is often created by the monochromatic fluorescence group that is invisible in the daytime. It can only show a fluorescent color under the excitation of a specific light source, and it is easy for counterfeiters to find alternatives to imitate the pattern. Therefore, giving color-rich and multi-dimensional anti-counterfeiting functions on the same anti-counterfeiting subject will have higher security and a specific challenge [17]. In order to achieve a high level of anti-replication, high-end anti-counterfeiting materials should be able to provide much optical information to carry unique information for customization [2].

In contrast, rare earth-doped fluorescent nanomaterials can avoid the problems of traditional fluorescent materials, such as wide emission band, low optical stability, and high biological toxicity, and have broad prospects in the application research of anti-counterfeiting ink and fingerprint detection [18–21]. Since rare earth elements have abundant energy level modes, rare earth-doped fluorescent nanomaterials can be divided into up-conversion (UC) and down-shift (DS) according to the luminescence mechanism [22,23]. Usually, the UC process can absorb low-energy photons and convert them into high-energy photons, while the DS refers to the opposite process [24]. UC fluorescent materials doped with rare earth ion pairs (such as Yb3+/Er3+ and Yb3+/Tm3+) can collect pump photons and emit multiple wavelengths [25]. Rare earth-doped (Tb3+ or Eu3+) DS fluorescent materials can also exhibit various fluorescent colors under short-wavelength excitation, such as red or green [22,26]. Recent studies have shown that NaREF4:Ln3+ nanocomposites have the advantages of low environmental toxicity, long fluorescence lifetime, rich and colorful fluorescence colors, and strong fluorescence emission peak specificity, which have been considered to have great application potential in the field of anti-counterfeiting [27,28]. Although monochromatic luminescent NaREF4:Ln3+ luminescent materials have been used in anti-counterfeiting, they are easily replicated due to their unique optical properties and simple preparation. Therefore, developing multimodal dynamic stimulus-responsive fluorescent anti-counterfeiting technology based on NaREF4:Ln3+ luminescent materials will show unique advantages in high-end anti-counterfeiting applications. In addition, it is still challenging to integrate multiple signals with different excitation wavelength dependence and temperature response properties into a single nanomaterial to construct multimodal anti-counterfeiting materials.

Given the above research background, a simple and universal strategy for the fabrication of multimodal fluorescent anti-counterfeiting hybrids with excellent luminous properties was proposed in this study. NaYF4:Yb3+/Tm3+ UC nanoparticles were loaded on YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb DS nanotubes, and then the Cit-Eu-TC (TC, tetracycline; Cit, citric acid) complex was modified to the surface of the composited nanotubes by using the abundant amino groups around the nanotubes. Finally, a multimodal luminescent hybrid material YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC was formed, which showed multiple anti-counterfeiting functions under three excitation channels (Scheme 1 ). Based on the fluorescence characteristics of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes, the three-mode fluorescent anti-counterfeiting material showed bright green fluorescence under the excitation of 254 nm. Under the excitation of 365 nm, the competition between TC and the coordination water molecules on rare earth Eu will enhance the red fluorescence of rare earth hydroxide nanotube composited nanomaterials. Under the 980 nm excitation light source, blue-violet fluorescence was observed. In addition, the material showed a dynamic anti-counterfeiting process under the excitation of 254 nm and 365 nm UV sources. Under the action of the display agent (TC solution) or the temperature change, the fluorescence color of the anti-counterfeiting pattern showed a dynamic change process, which further improved the multiple anti-counterfeiting abilities of the anti-counterfeiting material. Finally, the multimodal fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials were prepared into ink and hydrogel and applied to anti-counterfeiting. Only when the fluorescence colors or patterns excited by the combination of three different light sources (254 nm, 365 nm, and 980 nm) were entirely correct could the authenticity of anti-counterfeiting products be confirmed. It will increase the difficulty of replication and forgery to a large extent and show the tremendous anti-counterfeiting application potential of the material.

All reagents are analytically pure and can be used without further purification. Rare earth nitrate was purchased from Shanghai Annaiji Chemical Co., Ltd. Ammonium fluoride (NH4F) was purchased from Tianjin Comiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), sodium chloride (NaCl), ethylene glycol, citric acid (Cit), rare earth chloride and tetracycline (TC) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36–38%) and tris (hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris, 99%) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS), and corresponding element mapping were measured at 300 kV accelerated voltage using Tecnai-G2-F30. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) in the range of 5–80° was obtained by RigakuDmax 2400 CuKα radiation diffractometer. The solid powder was mixed with KBr particles with a wave number range of 4000–400 cm−1, and then the transparent sheet was pressed into the transparent sheet to determine the infrared spectrum of the material. The fluorescence spectrum was measured by a Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer.

YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb (Tb/(Tb + Ce + Y) molar ratio = 11.4%) nanotubes were prepared based on the existing methods [29]. Firstly, LnCl3 (Ln = Y, Ce, Tb) was dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare a solution with a 0.5 mol/L concentration. In the synthesis process, YCl3·6H2O (0.78 mL), CeCl3·7H2O (0.15 mL), TbCl3·6H2O (0.12 mL), and NH4F (0.975 mL, 1 mol/L) were dissolved in a mixed solvent of 21 mL ethanol and 36 mL ultrapure water. Then, 3 mL of PEI (10 wt %) was added to the solution under nitrogen protection for 5 min. Finally, the above solution was put into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. The precipitate obtained by centrifugation was washed with water and ethanol three times and then dried in a vacuum for further use.

An appropriate amount of rare earth nanotubes were added to the preparation process of up-conversion nanoparticles reported in the literature [30], and YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Specifically, YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb (100 mg) was dispersed in 25 mL of ethylene glycol, followed by adding 1.538 mmol NaCl and 1.5 mL PEI. After stirring for 30 min, the aqueous solution containing 0.78 mmol of YCl3·6H2O, 0.2 mmol of YbCl3·6H2O, 0.02 mmol of TmCl3·6H2O and 4 mmol NH4F was added to the above mixture. After stirring for 10 min, the obtained mixture was transferred to a PTFE autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The final YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm solid was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for further use. The synthesis process of pure NaYF4: Yb/Tm was similar to that of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm, except for the addition of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of multi-mode fluorescent anti-counterfeit nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm (100 mg) and activated Cit (100 mg) were dispersed in 25 mL of ethanol. After stirring for 24 h, the solid YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit was collected by centrifugation and re-dispersed in 20 mL of ethanol. After adding 50 mg of EuCl3·6H2O, the mixture was heated at 60 °C for 5 h. After washing and removing free Eu3+, the mixture of obtained YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu and TC (5 mg) in 10 mL of ethanol was stirred at room temperature for 4 h. The final product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol three times, and dried in a vacuum for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink and hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink was as follows. Firstly, 8 mL of ultrapure water, 24 mL of ethanol, and 8 mL of glycerol were added into 100 mL round-bottom flask and stirred for 1 h to make the mixture uniform and transparent. Secondly, YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC (100 mg) was added to the above mixture and ultrasonically mixed for 1 h to obtain three-channel anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink.

The preparation method of anti-counterfeiting hydrogel was as follows. 250 mg of agarose was dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water and heated at 100 °C for 1 h, followed by 2 mL of a water solution containing YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC (20 mg). After heating for 10 min, it was poured into the mold while it was heated and cooled naturally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, NaYF4:Yb/Tm had a uniform spherical structure with a particle size of about 17 nm. From the HRTEM image, the crystal spacing of 0.311 nm lattice stripes corresponding to the cubic NaYF4 (111) crystal plane was observed. Fig. 1b shows the typical TEM images of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes. The tube length was about 647 nm, and the wall thickness was about 12 nm, which was similar to that of hexagonal Y(OH)1·69F1.31 nanotubes reported previously [29]. The lattice fringes in the illustration of Fig. 1b indicated that the spacing of two different crystal planes at 0.321 nm and 0.298 nm were very consistent with the d spacings of (110) and (101) crystal planes of hexagonal Y(OH)3-xFx [30]. Fig. 1c and d shows the TEM images of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC. It can be clearly seen that almost all NaYF4:Yb/Tm up-conversion rare-earth nanoparticles were uniformly embedded in YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes. In addition, the outer wall of nanotubes became rougher after the outermost layer of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes was modified by rare earth Eu complexes. The lattice fringes are shown by HRTEM in Fig. 1c and d demonstrated that the three different crystal plane spacing of 0.321 nm, 0.298 nm, and 0.311 nm in the composites were very consistent with the d spacing of hexagonal Y(OH)3-xF, NaYF4, and NaYF4:Yb/Tm lattice planes, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 2 a–c, SEM results also showed that YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes had good dispersion before and after functionalization, while NaYF4:Yb/Tm up-conversion nanoparticles and Cit-Eu-TC complexes were uniformly loaded on YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes. The EDS-mapping diagram in Fig. 2d shows some important elements, such as F, Ce, Y, Yb, Tm, Tb, and Eu uniformly distributed on the nanotubes, indicating the successful preparation of the above multimodal fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials. EDX results also proved the existence of Yb, Tm, Eu, Tb, C, O, N and F elements in this multimodal fluorescent anti-counterfeiting material (Fig. S1), which further confirmed the successful synthesis of nanocomposites. PXRD diagrams in Fig. 3 a display sharp and strong peaks, indicating that the fluorescent nanotubes had good crystallinity. The diffraction peak (Fig. 3a, blue) was very consistent with that of the standard hexagonal phase Y(OH)1·69F1.31 (Fig. 3a black, JCPDS standard card 80–2006) [29]. Compared with the original nanotubes, the final YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC nanomaterial had obvious diffraction peaks on (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) crystal planes [31], indicating that NaYF4:Yb/Tm up-conversion nanoparticles were successfully loaded on YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes.

The XPS spectra in Fig. 3b (black line) illustrated that the obvious diffraction peaks at 159.8 eV, 395.6 eV, 685.9 eV, 885.8 eV, and 905.6 eV, 1242.4 eV and 1280.2 eV were attributed to Y3d, N1s, O1s, F1s, Ce3d, and Tb3d, respectively. Compared with YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb, YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC provided the diffraction peaks of Na 1s, Yb 4 d, and Tm 3 d, which meant that NaYF4:Yb/Tm up-conversion nanoparticles were successfully loaded on YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes. Fig. 3c shows the detailed Eu 3 d spectra of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC. Due to the multiple splitting of the spin-orbital j = 5/2 and 3/2 components of Eu, four peaks can be observed in the high-resolution spectra of Eu 3 d. The peaks at 1134.6 eV and 1164.4 eV belonged to Eu3+ 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively, while 1124.6 eV belonged to Eu2+ 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively [32]. The results in Fig. 3d and e indicated the characteristic peak of Tm 4 d was 174.6 eV, and that of Yb 4 d was 186.41 eV [33]. As shown in Fig. 3f, the Tb 3 d peak in YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC decreased, which may be due to the presence of some NaYF4:Yb/Tm up-conversion nanoparticles outside the nanotubes. Fig. S2 shows the detailed C 1s and N 1s spectra of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb and YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC. It can be observed that the C1s can be divided into five peaks, namely, CC peak at 284.56 eV, C–N/C–C peak at 284.85 eV, C–O peak at 285.99 eV, CN peak at 288.66 eV and CO peak at 289.22 eV [31,34]. Compared with YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb, the N1s high-resolution spectrum of the final composites showed a N–CO peak at 401.6 eV band [35]. Similarly, the Y 3 d spectra also displayed bimodal states of YF3 at 158.9 eV and 161.1 eV (Figs. S3a and 3b) [36]. The abovementioned surface element changes proved that NaYF4:Yb and Tm-Cit-Eu-TC complex were successfully coated on YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes. In addition, the characteristic peaks of Ce 3 d were fixed at 902.8 eV and 884.2 eV (Figs. S3c and 3d) [37], indicating no change in the surface bonding state of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes before and after functionalization.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared nanocomposite after Ce3+ doping was significantly enhanced. Since Ce3+ can act as the sensitizer which can absorb light energy and transfer energy to the codoped Tb3+ ions, resulting in bright green fluorescence of Tb3+ under 254 nm UV lamp (illustration in Fig. 4a) [29]. The characteristic emission peaks at 489, 545, 584, and 621 nm were attributed to the 5D4→7F J transition of Tb3+ [38]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity at 545 nm was stable in the temperature range of 5–40 °C under the excitation of 260 nm, which suggested the potential to become an anti-counterfeiting ink.

It can be seen from Fig. 4c that the luminescence intensity was significantly improved after the addition of TC. Under the excitation of 380 nm, the characteristic emission of Eu3+ (5D0) transition to the ground state (7F J , J = 1, 2, 3, 4) appeared at 592, 616, 650 and 696 nm, and the emission peak of 5D0→7F2 were the most prominent [39]. The enhancement mechanism of red fluorescence can be attributed to the following two aspects. On the one hand, TC can transfer absorbed energy to the coordinated Eu3+ through the antenna effect. Fig. 4d shows the energy transfer mechanism of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu under 380 nm excitation. The gap between the triplet energy level (T1) of TC (19,160 cm−1) and the energy range of Eu3+ (17,300 cm−1) were 1860 cm−1, meeting Latva ‘s rule [32]. On the other hand, TC with β-diketonate configuration showed more extraordinary coordination ability with Eu3+ than that of the coordinated water molecule. It can replace the coordinated water molecules and reduce the vibration coupling of O–H oscillator from water molecules, enhancing red fluorescence for Eu3+ emission. The numbers of coordinated water molecules can be determined by the lifetime of Eu(III) monitored at 616 nm (Fig. S4) according to the equation [40,41]:
q = 1.1
[
τ
H 2 O
− 1
−
τ
D 2 O
− 1
− 0.31
]
where τH2O and τD2O are the decay time in H2O and D2O buffers, respectively. After the addition of TC to YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu, the q value decreased from 9.01 to 5.02, further indicating that TC can replace the coordinated water molecules and transfer energy to Eu3+ efficiently under 365 nm channel.

The stimulus-responsive performance and mechanism of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu to TC were further studied. Fig. S5a indicated the fluorescence intensity of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu reached the maximum at pH = 7 after adding 10 μM of TC, which due to Eu3+ will partially come into being Eu(OH)2 precipitation under alkaline conditions, leading to a decrease of fluorescence intensity at 616 nm [42]. Fig. S5b illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu was completed within 2 min and reached a balance, which was stable. As shown in Figs. S6a and 6b, there was a good linear relationship between the amount of TC and the fluorescence intensity at 616 nm. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu with the increase of TC dosages were calculated according to their emission spectra (Table S1). While Figs. S6c and 6d indicated that YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu was expected to be used for actual dynamic anti-counterfeiting under TC stimulation.

Fluorescence intensity was studied at different temperatures (5–40 °C) to explore further the relationship between temperature and the sensitivity of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC. As provided in Fig. S7a, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 616 nm decreased with increasing temperature, which was consistent with the non-radiation relaxation and thermal inactivation caused by the vibration interaction between europium and the environment [43,44]. In the heating process, the normalized experimental data of I616 is shown in Fig. S7b. The solid red line was the fitting curve, which followed the following empirical function
(1)
I 616
= A
T 2
+ B T + C
where A = 5.40311 × 10−4，B = −0.04779，C = 1.23763, and correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.99656. Similarly, the value of I616 increased during cooling (Figs. S7c and 7d), and the regression equation was I 616 = 5.44748 × 10−4 T 2 – 0.04814T + 1.25356 (R2 = 0.9928). In addition, the fluorescence intensity (I616) was reversible for continuous heating and cooling in the temperature range of 5–40 °C (Fig. S8), indicating that YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC was expected to be used for the actual dynamic anti-counterfeiting under temperature stimulation.

The up-conversion emission spectrum of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu in Fig. 4e exhibited typical up-conversion fluorescence emission peaks at 450 nm, 475 nm, and 644 nm, which corresponded to the 1D2→3F4, 1G4→3H6 and 1G4→3F4 transitions of Tm3+, respectively [29,33]. Fig. 4f shows the possible energy transfer up-conversion mechanism diagram in YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu. Under 980 nm excitation, Yb3+ continuously absorbed 980 nm photons and transferred energy to 3H5 , 3F2(3F3), and 1G4 levels in Tm3+ ions. Subsequently, the non-radiative multi-phonon decay of 3H5/3F4 also filled the 3F4 level, and the non-radiative relaxation led to the filling of the 3F4 level. Finally, the 1G4/3H6 transition produced blue emission centered at 475 nm (Tm3+, 1G4→3H6), and relatively weak red emission at 644 nm (Tm3+, 1G4→3F4) and 700 nm (Tm3+, 3F3→3H6). Since the intensity of the blue emission peak was significantly higher than that of the red emission peak, YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC displayed dominant blue-purple color under 980 nm laser irradiation (as shown in the illustration in Fig. 4e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on three-channel static anti-counterfeiting performance
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the three-mode fluorescence characteristics of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC, it can be used in the field of patterned anti-counterfeiting. Anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink was prepared by dispersing YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC in a certain proportion of water, glycerin and ethanol mixed solution. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a green fluorescent swan pattern appeared under 254 nm UV lamp; under the excitation of 365 nm, the red fluorescent swan pattern can be seen; when irradiated by 980 nm laser, blue-violet fluorescence appeared in the swan pattern. In addition, the anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink can be combined with green commercial ink to increase the difficulty of forgery. As shown in Fig. 5b–e, all the patterns showed green fluorescence under the excitation of 254 nm; under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, the pattern part of the anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink showed red fluorescence, and its fluorescence color remained unchanged; when irradiated by 980 nm laser, the part drawn by this anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink showed blue and purple fluorescence. In short, the authenticity of anti-counterfeiting products can be confirmed only when the fluorescence colors under three different excitations of 254 nm, 365 nm, and 980 nm were entirely correct. This strategy provided a new idea for designing and applying multimodal high-end anti-counterfeiting materials.

In addition, as the above fluorescent ink existed in the form of liquid, it was easy to splash out and was not easy to store and carry. Therefore, it was urgent to develop environmentally friendly, easy-to-store, and portable luminescent ink. In recent years, luminescent gels have attracted significant attention from many researchers and have been widely used in anti-counterfeiting [45–48]. This study used YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC to prepare a series of fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns with different shapes of agarose hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 6 , these hydrogel patterns were white and did not glow under sunlight. Under 254 nm light, the pattern emits bright green fluorescence; under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, the hydrogel pattern showed red fluorescence; while under the irradiation of 980 nm excitation light source, it presented blue-violet fluorescence. Using this three-mode multi-channel luminescence form, YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC agarose hydrogel pattern showed great anti-counterfeiting application potential.

The above experimental results illustrated that YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu not only had an excellent response to TC in the 365 nm channel but also had a reversible stimulus-responsive to temperature after the addition of developer. Therefore, the fluorescence pattern prepared by this fluorescent material had specific dynamic anti-counterfeiting characteristics. As shown in Fig. 7 , the prepared aircraft and pigeon patterns showed green fluorescence in 254 channels but no fluorescence in 365 channels. Therefore, these patterns only showed green fluorescence under co-irradiation at 254 nm and 365 nm. However, a small amount of TC sprayed on the surface of the pattern led to red fluorescence in the 365 nm channel. The pattern under 254 nm and 365 nm co-irradiation showed orange fluorescence due to the superposition of red and green fluorescence. More interestingly, when assisted by a hot air gun, the red fluorescence of the pattern in the 365 nm channel was quenched, and the original red fluorescence appeared as the temperature decreased. Therefore, under the co-irradiation of 254 nm and 365 nm, these patterns exhibited dynamic changes from green to orange under the combined action of TC and temperature. The combination of this dynamic stimulus-responsive anti-counterfeiting feature and the above three-channel static anti-counterfeiting feature might bring about multi-mode static-dynamic anti-counterfeiting performance, significantly improving the difficulty of forgery and was expected to be widely used in the field of confidential information and file anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, PEI-coated cerium-terbium doped yttrium hydroxide fluoride nanotubes were prepared by the hydrothermal synthesis method in this research. NaYF4:Yb/Tm UC nanoparticles were loaded on the surface of YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb nanotubes and the rare earth complexes were chelated onto the surface of the composited material by covalent grafting. Finally, a multimodal fluorescent anti-counterfeiting material YHF:Ce/11.4%Tb@NaYF4:Yb/Tm-Cit-Eu-TC was formed. Based on its excellent fluorescence properties, this novel luminescent material can prepare anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink and anti-counterfeiting hydrogel for three-mode static anti-counterfeiting. The hidden three-mode anti-counterfeiting pattern showed bright green, red, and blue-violet fluorescence under 254 nm, 365 nm, and 980 nm excitation, respectively. More importantly, the multimodal fluorescent anti-counterfeiting material displayed excellent dynamic anti-counterfeiting properties under the co-excitation of 254 nm and 365 nm UV sources. Under the stimulation of the display agent (TC solution) or temperature, the fluorescence color of the anti-counterfeiting pattern presented a dynamic change process, which further improved the multiple anti-counterfeiting abilities of the anti-counterfeiting material. Only when the fluorescence colors under 254 nm, 365 nm, and 980 nm excitation and under developer and temperature stimulation were entirely correct, the authenticity of anti-counterfeiting products can be confirmed, which significantly increased the difficulty of simulation and forgery and improved the security of anti-counterfeiting.

Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation. Tinghui Zhu: Visualization, Investigation, Software. Xiangzhen Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation, Software. Dan Zhao: Software, Validation. Yongxin Li: Formal analysis, Software. Lina Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ning Bi: Software, Validation. Jian Gou: Formal analysis, Software, Methodology. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1904168, No. 51773052, and No. 21805071), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (222300420047), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2), and the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2022.119647.
